For practitioners, the narrower claim is still actionable:
those who want to keep the familiar CP-promising-zone SSR machinery
can recover calibrated T1E by replacing the $z$-test final with a
Bayesian posterior threshold or with an exactly-calibrated critical
count; alternatively, they can adopt a Bayesian PP design end-to-end.


# Discussion

**Why the Mehta–Pocock guarantee fails for single-arm binary trials.**
The analytic Type I error control of @mehta2011 is inherited from the
inverse-normal combination test of @lehmacher1999 and @muller2001,
which combines pre- and post-interim test statistics on a *continuous*
$z$-statistic scale. The combination weights are fixed before the
trial and do not depend on the SSR decision, which preserves the joint
distribution of the combined statistic under $H_0$. In single-arm
binary trials, the interim "test statistic" is a binomial response
count with $n_\text{int} + 1$ possible values, and the final-analysis
$z$-test is applied to the *integer* total response count. The
discrete critical count $x_\text{crit}(N)$ is the smallest integer
with $z \geq z_\alpha$, so the actual fixed-$N$ rejection probability
under $H_0$ generally differs from the nominal $\alpha$ by a *signed*
amount that depends on $(p_0, N)$, it can be either upward (e.g.,
the base setting at $p_0 = 0.23, N = 84$) or downward (e.g., the
$p_0 = 0.10, N = 55$ setting in @fig-generalizability). The promising-zone SSR
moves trials between such finite-$N$ tests, with their own
opposite-sign discreteness biases, in a way that depends on the
$\text{CP}_L$ threshold and the realised $x_\text{int}$. The
resulting T1E behaviour is design-specific and irregular: it is not
a numerical artefact, but a structural consequence of running an
integer-grid test at finite $N$ combined with $N$-changing SSR, and
it cannot be controlled by a fixed-$\text{CP}_L$ rule that
generalises across designs.

**Practical implication for trial designers.** Practitioners using the
CP promising-zone SSR for single-arm binary trials should not rely on
the analytic Type I error guarantee from @mehta2011. In our base
setting no $\text{CP}_L$ on the grid achieves
$\text{T1E} \leq \alpha$; in other design configurations (@fig-generalizability),
the picture is mixed, some $\text{CP}_L$ values control T1E, others
do not, and there is no consistent rule for which. "Tuning"
$\text{CP}_L$ on a fixed grid is therefore unreliable: the
discreteness bias dominates the SSR effect and changes sign across
designs, so a $\text{CP}_L$ that controls T1E in one configuration
may not control it in another. Practitioners who must nonetheless
use this design should report exact T1E (or simulated T1E with
adequate precision) across their full $\text{CP}_L$ grid for their
*specific* design parameters, treat the
worst-case T1E across plausible design perturbations as the operating
characteristic, and not extrapolate calibration from one design to
another.

**Bayesian PP alternative.** Where calibrated Type I error control
is required, the Bayesian PP SSR design with separate
$\gamma_\text{int}$ and $\gamma_\text{final}$ thresholds offers an
attractive alternative. Exact Type I error is monotone in
$\gamma_\text{final}$, and although it remains stepwise (success
ultimately depends on integer counts), the calibration surface is
far less irregular than the $\text{CP}_L$ surface, enumeration
over the $\gamma_\text{final}$ grid yields a clean smallest-value
that controls T1E, with no need to navigate sign-changing
discreteness bias. Power and operating characteristics are
competitive with the best-calibrated CP design (when one exists)
and dominate it when CP cannot be calibrated.

**Regulatory implication.** This is consistent with the FDA 2019 adaptive
design guidance [@fda2019], which requires demonstration of Type I
error control, analytically where possible, otherwise via simulation.
For the one-look single-arm binary design we study, exact enumeration
is in fact available (we use it for every headline number above) and
provides stronger evidence than simulation can; for more complex
adaptive designs where enumeration becomes impractical (multiple
interim looks, continuous endpoints with adaptive boundaries),
simulation remains the standard route. Our results imply that any
analysis, exact or simulated, must evaluate the entire $\text{CP}_L$
grid the design might use, not a single point.

**Limitations.** The designs studied here have a single interim look,
a binary ORR endpoint, a Jeffreys non-informative prior, and a
one-sided normal-approximation $z$-test for the final analysis; our
exact enumeration is specific to that combination. The qualitative
claim, that the discrete $z$-test critical count produces a
*design-specific* (and signed) rounding bias at finite $N$, and that
$N$-changing SSR rearranges these biases in a way that defies
fixed-rule calibration, should generalise to other discrete
endpoints where the final-analysis test reduces to integer-grid
arithmetic. An exact (Clopper–Pearson) final analysis would push T1E
uniformly below $\alpha$ at the cost of obscuring the SSR-driven
boundary-crossing effects we characterise; the choice between
approximate and exact tests is a separate design decision that
practitioners should make explicitly. Multiple interim looks,
informative priors (e.g., MAP), and a fully-symmetric
Bayesian-vs-frequentist comparison (in which the two designs share a
final analysis rule and differ only in their interim decision logic)
are natural directions for follow-up work; for multiple-look designs
the simulator-based `run_*_scenario` framework in this manuscript is
the natural extension path, since exact enumeration becomes
combinatorially expensive once the look count grows.


# Conclusion

The Mehta–Pocock conditional-power promising-zone SSR with a one-sided
$z$-test final analysis cannot be calibrated to a target Type I error
in single-arm binary trials by a rule that generalises across designs.
In a representative oncology setting ($p_0 = 0.23$, $p_1 = 0.35$,
$N_\text{init} = 84$, $n_\text{int} = 65$, $N_\text{max} = 200$), exact
Type I error sits above the nominal $\alpha$ at every $\text{CP}_L$
on the grid; across other design configurations, the discrete
$z$-test critical count's rounding bias changes sign, so
$\text{CP}_L$ values that control T1E in one configuration may not
control it in another. The overall T1E surface is too irregular to
support a fixed-rule calibration. The partial symmetric comparison
in Section 4.6 shows that switching the CP design's final analysis
to a Bayesian posterior threshold is **sufficient** to restore
calibratable T1E, even with the same CP-zone SSR logic; the
discrete $z$-test final is therefore a *sufficient cause* of the
inflation. Because we do not implement the complementary variant
(PP-driven SSR with a $z$-test final), we cannot rule out an
additional contribution from the SSR-logic itself. Practitioners
who want calibrated Type I error control therefore have two
practical paths: replace the $z$-test final with a calibrated
final-analysis rule (Bayesian posterior threshold or an
exactly-calibrated critical count chosen so that the fixed-$N$
test attains nominal $\alpha$) while keeping the CP-zone SSR
machinery, or adopt a Bayesian PP SSR design end-to-end. Bayesian
PP SSR is a particularly attractive alternative because its exact
Type I error is monotone in $\gamma_\text{final}$ and calibrates
directly by enumeration, the curve is still stepwise (success
ultimately depends on integer response counts), but the
calibration surface is far less irregular than the CP_L surface.


# Code and Data Availability

All code used in this paper is released as a public R/Quarto repository,
presented anonymously for double-blind peer review at
<https://anonymous.4open.science/r/CP-SSR-Binary-Trials-8DB8/>. The
non-anonymized public GitHub repository will be disclosed in the
camera-ready version after acceptance. The repository contains the Quarto
manuscript source (`CP_PromisingZone_SSR_TypeIError.qmd`),
the bibliography (`references.bib`), and an R script (`R/analysis.R`)
that defines the exact-enumeration helpers (`cp_oc_exact`, `bayes_oc_exact`,
`cp_oc_post_final`, `fixed_n_t1e`) and simulator-based fallback functions
(`sim_trial_cp`, `sim_trial_bayes`, `run_*_scenario`). The manuscript is
fully reproducible from the `.qmd` source with R $\geq$ 4.4 and the
following CRAN packages: `tidyverse`, `gt` ($\geq$ 1.0.0), `scales`,
`patchwork`, `glue`, and `latex2exp`. No external data are used: every
numerical result is computed from the exact enumeration code in
`R/analysis.R`.


# References

:::{#refs}
:::


<!--
Session info is omitted from the journal-submission PDF. The exact R
version and package versions used to render this manuscript are pinned
in the public repository's `R/analysis.R` and reproducible via the
included `.qmd` source. To re-enable session info during development,
delete the surrounding HTML comment markers.

```{r session}
#| eval: false
sessionInfo()
```
-->
